{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"Ahafo 🟡: Ahafo demonstrates mostly stable testing across the period but with recurring modest dips. Surpluses appear periodically, especially in 2024–2025, but disruptions during earlier years keep the region in the moderate-performance category. Overall, testing is close to expected but not fully stable.

North East 🔴: North East shows strong seasonal responsiveness but marked volatility. Surpluses during peaks are offset by clear disruptions during low-season troughs. Although the region performs well during high-demand periods, inconsistency across seasons places it in the yellow band.

Northern 🟡: Northern Region handles high testing volumes with relatively strong performance but exhibits multiple disruptions in 2022–2023. Improvements in 2024–2025 are visible, but instability during transition months prevents it from earning a green rating.

Oti 🟢: Oti is one of the most stable performers. Testing aligns closely with expected trends, with mild fluctuations but no extended disruptions. Surpluses in peak seasons further reinforce consistency. Overall, the region demonstrates strong testing reliability.

Savannah 🟢: Savannah shows significant volatility with repeated peaks followed by substantial dips, particularly in 2021–2023. Although improvement is visible in 2024–2025, the region experiences more disruptions than surpluses across the whole period. Service consistency remains a challenge.

Upper East 🔴: Upper East has high testing volumes but experiences prolonged disruptions during multiple years, especially 2022–2023. Although some recovery appears later, persistent dips below expected thresholds and recurrent instability justify a red rating.

Upper West 🔴: Upper West displays recurring disruptions across both peak and low seasons, with several years of performance below expected trends. Although some surpluses occur, they are insufficient to offset sustained volatility. The region requires targeted support.

Western 🟡: Western Region is among the most stable performers, with minimal extreme fluctuations and consistent testing volumes across years. Small deviations occur but do not significantly affect overall performance. Testing closely follows expected seasonal patterns."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Disruptions and surpluses in malaria testing services, Regional Performance"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","markdown":"The pattern of positive HIV cases linked to care shows generally stable performance over time, but with clear pockets of both surpluses and disruptions when actual volumes diverge from expected trends. Early in 2020, the system experienced notable surpluses, with actual linkage volumes exceeding expected levels, possibly reflecting recovery from earlier reporting lags or intensified case-finding efforts. These surpluses re-emerge in late 2020 into early 2021, where actual linkage volumes again rise above expected levels, signalling strengthened continuity of care and program responsiveness.\n\nFrom mid-2022 into early 2023, the chart shows distinct disruptions, where actual linkage volumes dip below expected levels for several consecutive months. These disruptions may reflect temporary operational constraints, shifts in testing volumes, or facility-level service interruptions. {"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":true,"figureAdditionalScale":0.9,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":true,"hideFigureSubCaption":true,"hideFigureFootnote":true,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"37b275b6-952c-4cdf-95aa-dbf886f01bf2","moduleId":"m001","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}},{"type":"text","span":5,"markdown":"The completeness of reported malaria data from 2020–2025 is generally low across all indicators and regions, with every value falling below the 80% threshold. Reporting on babies receiving ARV prophylaxis is especially weak, with most regions submitting fewer than one-third of expected reports, and Upper East showing the lowest completeness at only 15.8%.  Similarly, linkage into HIV care, HIV testing among the population, and HIV testing coverage show substantial gaps, with completeness typically ranging between 30–60%. Even regions that tend to perform better on other health indicators, such as Greater Accra, Volta, and Bono, still fall short of acceptable completeness standards in this dataset. The consistently low levels across all regions and indicators suggest major challenges in routine reporting systems for malaria-related HIV indicators, including delayed submissions, non-reporting facilities, or systemic issues with DHIMS2 data entry workflows. \nThis level of incompleteness limits the reliability of the dataset for monitoring performance, identifying service gaps, or guiding interventions, and highlights the need for targeted data-quality strengthening efforts at facility and district levels.","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}]],"useFooter":false,"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Completeness of Reported HIV Data, 2020 - 2025"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":true,"hideFigureSubCaption":true,"hideFigureFootnote":true,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"1d35f841-b978-4551-846f-9b88f13e3aed","moduleId":"m001","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}},{"type":"text","span":4,"markdown":"The malaria case-management indicators show a mixed picture of strong performance in some areas and notable gaps in others. Most regions demonstrate relatively good adherence to test-before-treat policy, with the proportion of suspected malaria cases tested generally above 90%, indicating strong diagnostic coverage across the country. Treatment of confirmed cases with ACTs is also fairly high in several regions -particularly Bono East, Eastern, Upper West, Volta, and Western North- where coverage often exceeds 85%. However, some regions consistently fall below the 80% threshold, including Ashanti, Greater Accra, and parts of Northern Ghana, pointing to gaps that may be linked to stockouts, delayed care seeking, or incomplete reporting.\n\nAdmissions for malaria among both children under five and older individuals are generally within the expected range.","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Completeness of Reported Malaria Data, 2020 - 2025"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":true,"hideFigureSubCaption":true,"hideFigureFootnote":true,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"1106f29e-fcf0-43de-80e5-0a036d86e365","moduleId":"m001","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}},{"type":"text","span":5,"markdown":"Data completeness across RMNCAH-N indicators shows mixed but generally improving performance, with notable variation across regions and indicators. Completeness for postnatal care (PNC1 for mothers) is mostly within the 80–89% range across the country, with only Upper West falling below 70%, signalling persistent reporting gaps in that region. For haemoglobin checks at ≥36 weeks of pregnancy, performance is weaker overall: most regions fall below 80%, highlighting systemic under-reporting and potential gaps in third-trimester ANC documentation. Savannah, Upper West, North East, and Eastern show the lowest completeness, suggesting structural or staffing-related challenges in ANC reporting. In contrast, haemoglobin checks at ANC registration demonstrate the strongest completeness levels nationally, with several regions, Ahafo, Bono East, and Western, achieving over 90%, indicating better consistency in early-pregnancy ANC data capture. However, a cluster of regions, including Central, Northern, Upper West, and Western North, remain below 85%, reflecting opportunities to strengthen facility documentation practices. Overall, the pattern suggests that early ANC indicators are better reported than late-pregnancy indicators","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Completeness of Reported RMNCAH-N Data, 2020 - 2025"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"North East 🔴— North East experienced a clear and persistent disruption in treatment services during this period. From early 2025 into the middle of the year, actual service use consistently trailed below expected levels, signaling operational or supply challenges that were not quickly resolved. Small surpluses appeared occasionally, but they were short-lived and insufficient to counterbalance the prolonged underperformance. The region’s trajectory indicates a substantial gap in treatment delivery that requires investigation and corrective support.

Northern 🔴— In the Northern Region, treatment services showed a pattern of instability with significant dips beginning in early 2025. Although performance improved marginally mid-year, the region struggled to maintain consistency and often fell below expected values. The lack of sustained recovery suggests ongoing operational strain, possibly linked to the broader challenges previously noted in the region’s health service delivery. Performance during this period skews more toward disruption than stability.

Oti 🟡— Oti demonstrated a mixed performance, with treatment levels alternating between slightly above and slightly below expected trends. While small disruptions were visible in the early months of 2025, they were neither deep nor prolonged. The region stabilised as the year progressed, closing the observation period with treatment volumes that aligned reasonably well with expectations. The overall picture is one of moderate variability but generally acceptable service continuity.


Upper East 🟡— Upper East exhibited a sustained decline in treatment service delivery, beginning in late 2024 and continuing throughout the first half of 2025. The region consistently failed to meet expected treatment levels, with no notable periods of recovery. This prolonged underperformance points to structural or supply-side challenges that may be affecting access or utilization. The magnitude and duration of the disruption make Upper East one of the weakest performers during this period.

Upper West 🔴— Upper West showed moderate performance challenges across late 2024 into mid-2025. Although there were some periods of stability and isolated surpluses, treatment volumes dipped repeatedly below expected trends, particularly during the early part of 2025. The disruptions were not as prolonged as those seen in Upper East or North East, but the pattern indicates systemic strain rather than isolated fluctuations. Recovery toward Q3 2025 was partial and inconsistent.


Western North 🟡— Western North encountered persistent challenges in meeting expected treatment volumes across the entire period. From late 2024 well into 2025, service levels remained below expected trends with only isolated and short-lived surpluses. The absence of a sustained rebound points to chronic disruptions in treatment delivery, potentially linked to supply, staffing, or service access issues. The region’s performance reflects one of the most consistently disrupted patterns nationally."}]]}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":true,"hideFigureSubCaption":true,"hideFigureFootnote":true,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"eec4952d-afc6-42c5-a544-52ec7935b428","moduleId":"m002","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}},{"type":"text","span":5,"markdown":"The completeness adjustment reveals substantial upward corrections across all HIV service indicators, indicating that a considerable portion of service volumes was initially under-reported. The largest proportional increases are seen in babies on ARV prophylaxis, where nearly all regions recorded extreme adjustments, ranging from about 85% in Bono to over 400% in the Upper East, suggesting major data gaps in routine reporting for PMTCT services. Similarly, linkage into HIV care shows large upward shifts in several regions, especially the North East, Oti, and Savannah, where adjustments exceed 100%, pointing to widespread under-capture of entry-point data. For population-level HIV testing and positivity, the adjustments are more moderate but still significant: many regions required increases of 20–60%, with Upper East, Upper West, and Savannah surpassing 100% for HIV-positive cases. These patterns imply that both testing and treatment cascades were substantially underestimated in the raw data. Even the indicator with the smallest corrections, Number Tested for HIV (Population), still shows increases of 15–80% across most regions. {"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"Ahafo 🟡: In Ahafo, malaria treatment volumes for older children and adults remained largely aligned with expected trends throughout late 2024, with only minor fluctuations. As the system moved into 2025, actual service use stayed close to seasonal expectations, showing neither pronounced surpluses nor significant disruptions. Overall, the region maintained a steady pattern of delivery, suggesting stable facility operations and consistent treatment-seeking behaviour.

North East 🔴: North East displayed relatively stable performance in late 2024, with actual service volumes tracking expected levels despite minor dips. In early to mid-2025, however, a small downturn emerged, marked by short-lived disruptions that brought actual volumes slightly below expected trends. While these deviations were not severe, they signal emerging pressures on service continuity that may warrant closer monitoring.

Northern 🔴: The Northern Region showed a generally predictable seasonal pattern through late 2024, but early 2025 brought a measurable decline in treatment volumes relative to expected levels. Although not extreme, this downward shift persisted across several months, indicating a meaningful disruption. Given Northern’s large population and persistent service demand, this trend suggests potential challenges such as stockouts, reduced facility attendance, or operational delays.

Oti 🟡: Oti maintained stable malaria treatment volumes through the end of 2024, aligning well with expected patterns. Entering 2025, the region experienced intermittent but noticeable dips, particularly around mid-year, where disruptions lowered service use below expected levels. The pattern suggests emerging inconsistencies in service access or supply availability that were not present earlier in the period.

Savannah 🔴: Savannah exhibited moderate surpluses in late 2024 before transitioning into a more erratic pattern in 2025. Although treatment volumes remained generally close to seasonal expectations, small disruptions appeared mid-2025, indicating possible interruptions in service delivery. Despite these declines, Savannah’s performance remained comparatively more stable than several neighbouring regions.

Upper East 🟡: Upper East showed relatively weak performance from late 2024 into 2025, with multiple months of actual service use falling below expected levels. The region experienced recurring disruptions across the first half of 2025, representing one of the clearest signs of sustained underperformance. This persistent gap highlights a potential lapse in supply chains, reporting reliability, or care-seeking patterns requiring follow-up.

Upper West 🔴: Upper West ended 2024 with service use closely following expected levels; however, early 2025 saw disruptions that kept actual volumes consistently below trend. Some recovery appeared mid-2025, but the rebound did not fully restore performance to expected levels. The repeated shortfalls suggest structural or logistical constraints affecting treatment continuity during this period.

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1.25,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"🟦 Suspected & Tested Malaria Cases

Suspected malaria cases in 2025 closely mirror the historical seasonal pattern observed from 2020–2024. Testing volumes have consistently matched suspected case volumes, demonstrating stable diagnostic capacity nationwide.

No signs of reduction in community health-seeking or facility-level diagnostic performance in 2025. The strong alignment between suspected and tested cases suggests that test-before-treat policy adherence remains intact.

🟩 Treatment Services (Under 5 Years & 5+ Years)

Both treatment pathways show normal performance up to mid-2025. From July to September 2025, treatment volumes fall below expected levels for both children under five and older age groups. The disruption is visible across multiple months and does not reflect typical seasonal fluctuations. Since suspected and tested cases remained stable, the drop in treatment cannot be explained by changes in disease burden or diagnostic trends.

🟥 Interpretation of the Treatment Gap

The July–Sept 2025 decline signals a specific disruption in treatment service delivery, not in detection or reporting. Potential drivers that require investigation. The synchronized drop across both age categories strengthens the likelihood of a systemic or supply-chain–related issue rather than a regional or age-specific anomaly."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Malaria – Key Takeaways (Jan 2020 – Sept 2025)"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"Ahafo 🟡 — In Ahafo, HIV testing volumes had been below expected levels for a long stretch from 2021 through mid-2024, but the trend turns positive from late 2024. Testing rises steadily and eventually exceeds expected levels into early–mid 2025, signalling a meaningful recovery. By mid-2025, the region shows a clear surplus, suggesting strengthened outreach, improved test kit availability, or renewed case-finding momentum.

North East 🟡 — North East remains a low-volume region, and the testing pattern from late 2024 through 2025 is relatively stable with modest fluctuations. While there is no severe disruption, the region does not show strong surpluses either. Testing remains broadly aligned with expected levels, though the small rise in early 2025 suggests some service strengthening.

Northern 🔴— Northern Region shows a slight downturn in testing activity toward late 2024, dipping below expected levels, but this short disruption is followed by a stabilization in early 2025. By mid-year, testing performance returns closer to expected levels, though the region does not achieve strong surpluses. Performance is moderate and somewhat fragile.

Oti 🟡 — Oti continues its characteristic oscillations, with several small dips below expected values from late 2024 into 2025. Though the declines are not sharp, they represent persistent instability rather than a clear improvement. Testing levels do recover intermittently, but performance remains inconsistent.

Savannah 🟢 — Savannah’s HIV testing pattern between late 2024 and mid-2025 is relatively flat. Testing stays close to expected levels, neither dropping significantly nor showing strong recovery. The region demonstrates stable but unremarkable performance without clear surpluses.

Upper East 🟢 — Upper East experiences noticeable disruptions beginning in late 2024 and continuing into 2025, with prolonged periods where testing lags below expected levels. This underperformance is more pronounced than in most other regions, suggesting supply-chain issues, workforce gaps, or disruptions in routine testing points. Recovery is limited by Sept 2025.

Upper West 🟢 — Upper West shows a period of weaker performance in late 2024 where testing dips below expected levels. Early 2025 remains subdued, but a slight recovery appears mid-year. However, the rebound is small, and overall performance remains modest and slightly below expectations.

Western 🟢 — Western Region maintains generally stable testing through late 2024 with brief dips. In early–mid 2025, testing levels improve and begin to approach expected volumes again. While there is still volatility, the overall direction trends toward recovery, indicating steadying service delivery.

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"Ahafo – 🟡 Mixed/Variable: Ahafo shows a generally steady trend in suspected malaria cases, staying close to expected levels across most months. North East – 🟡 Mixed/Variable: North East displays pronounced seasonal swings, with sharp rises around Q2–Q3 almost every year and noticeable drops afterward. This region experiences both surplus and disruption periods, reflecting a combination of seasonal malaria peaks and possible variations in care-seeking or testing capacity during low-transmission months. 

Northern – 🟡 Mixed/Variable: The Northern Region exhibits predictable seasonal peaks but alternating periods of surplus and disruption. Surpluses typically emerge mid-year, reflecting expected seasonal increases in suspected malaria. However, noticeable dips below expected levels occur in multiple years, especially around Q1 and late Q4, hinting at possible disruptions.

Oti – 🟢 Surplus-Leaning: Oti stands out for consistently meeting or exceeding expected suspected malaria cases. Surpluses appear frequently during peak transmission windows, and disruptions, when they occur, are minor and short. 

Upper East – 🔴 Disruption-Leaning: Upper East presents a more concerning profile, with more dips below expected values than surpluses across the entire 2020–2025 period. Although seasonal trends still appear, actual volumes consistently lag behind expected service use projections. 

Upper West – 🟡 Mixed/Variable: Upper West shows clear seasonality with mid-year peaks, but performance fluctuates around expected trends. Some years show strong surpluses, while others show notable dips. The variability suggests a system affected by external conditions (rainfall patterns, flooding, mobility constraints) combined with facility-level operational variations.



Western – ⚫ Stable/Neutral: Western Region performs close to expected levels throughout the review period. Deviations from projected volumes are minimal, with only small surpluses or disruptions appearing sporadically. The largest proportional increases are seen in babies on ARV prophylaxis, where nearly all regions recorded extreme adjustments, ranging from about 85% in Bono to over 400% in the Upper East, suggesting major data gaps in routine reporting for PMTCT services. Similarly, linkage into HIV care shows large upward shifts in several regions, especially the North East, Oti, and Savannah, where adjustments exceed 100%, pointing to widespread under-capture of entry-point data. For population-level HIV testing and positivity, the adjustments are more moderate but still significant: many regions required increases of 20–60%, with Upper East, Upper West, and Savannah surpassing 100% for HIV-positive cases. These patterns imply that both testing and treatment cascades were substantially underestimated in the raw data. Even the indicator with the smallest corrections, Number Tested for HIV (Population), still shows increases of 15–80% across most regions. {"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"🟢 Overall HIV service trends remain broadly stable across testing, positivity, and linkage to care, with quarterly patterns in 2024–2025 following historical expectations.  Testing volumes have recovered from the disruptions seen in 2022–2023, and positivity levels remain consistent with long-term patterns.


🟡 Short, temporary disruptions continue to appear in specific parts of the cascade, especially during mid-2022 to early-2023. These include dips in testing, positivity, and linkage-to-care volumes falling below expected levels for a few months at a time. While not prolonged, they signal operational fluctuations at facility level.

🔴 Data completeness for HIV indicators remains critically low across all regions, with most indicators falling far below the 80% completeness benchmark. Reporting gaps, especially for ARV prophylaxis for newborns, population HIV testing, and linkage to care, significantly limit the reliability of the dataset and complicate interpretation of performance trends.

🟡 Regional performance shows improvement in some areas but still exhibits substantial variability. Regions such as Greater Accra, Bono, Western, and Volta perform relatively better in testing and linkage trends but still show major data completeness gaps. Regions like Upper East, Oti, and Northern consistently under-report key HIV indicators.

🟢 Despite data limitations, linkage-to-care volumes demonstrate resilience and generally align with expected patterns through 2024 and 2025, indicating stable continuity-of-care efforts at national level.

⚫ Overall interpretation should be made cautiously due to persistent reporting weaknesses, which likely obscure true service coverage, mask facility-level disruptions, and highlight the need for systematic strengthening of DHIMS2 reporting workflows."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"HIV Service Delivery: Key Takeaways"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":true,"hideFigureSubCaption":true,"hideFigureFootnote":true,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"78328b06-0d42-42d8-abef-fb3ccd4fa9a3","moduleId":"m002","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}},{"type":"text","span":5,"markdown":"The completeness-adjusted RMNCAH-N service volumes show that most regions experienced modest increases (generally 2–6%) across ANC1, ANC4, institutional deliveries, postnatal care, and live births once reporting gaps were corrected. \nA few regions stand out with stronger gains: Ashanti, Upper West, Western North, and Savannah consistently show higher percentage increases (5–9%), suggesting that these areas had substantial under-reporting in the original DHIMS2 data. {"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1.25,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"Across the continuum of maternal health services, the disruption analysis shows a generally resilient system with short-lived fluctuations rather than sustained declines. 
ANC1 remained highly stable throughout the period, with only brief and mild dips below expected levels, indicating that first-contact antenatal care was consistently maintained even during periods of broader health system stress. ANC4, which is more sensitive to repeated service contact, also demonstrated strong stability with only small patches of surplus or minor disruptions; this suggests that once women entered ANC, many were able to continue their follow-up visits despite external pressures. 
Delivery services followed a similar pattern, seasonal fluctuations persisted, but the service never sustained deep or prolonged disruptions. Instead, occasional surpluses in late 2024 and 2025 indicate improved access or reporting completeness during those months. PNC services mirrored the delivery pattern, with stable utilisation and only a modest surplus emerging in 2025, again hinting at improved postpartum follow-up or data completeness. 
Finally, live births showed the highest consistency of all indicators, with values tracking very closely to expected patterns and only a notable surplus in early–mid 2025. Taken together, these trends highlight that the core maternal health service platform, ANC initiation, ANC completion, delivery care, and postnatal follow-up, remained largely robust across the five-year period, with no evidence of systemic collapse. The improvements seen in 2025 across multiple indicators likely reflect overall recovery, strengthened service continuity, and enhanced facility reporting."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Key Highlights - RMNCAH-N Service Delivery"}}

Around this peak, surpluses appear, where observed treatment volumes exceed the expected seasonal baseline, likely reflecting improved care-seeking, intensified case detection, or seasonal transmission peaks. A strong surplus period occurs again in mid-2024, with treatment volumes rising well above expectations. The gap between the two representing people who are not successfully linked to care narrows and widens at different points, providing insight into the system’s performance.\n\nEarly 2020 shows the largest discrepancies, especially in Q2 and Q3, where linkage to care drops sharply while positive tests remain relatively high. This suggests that COVID-19 disruptions significantly affected post-test linkage processes more than testing volumes themselves.","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Comparison Between HIV-Positive Cases and Those Linked to Care"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":true,"hideFigureSubCaption":true,"hideFigureFootnote":true,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"563dc525-a6fb-4b58-94a9-8a910985f687","moduleId":"m003","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}}],[{"type":"text","markdown":"In contrast to the national performance, the Northern Region demonstrates a distinct shift in performance, particularly in 2025. Between 2020 and 2022, the region exhibited strong linkage outcomes, with the number of clients linked to care closely mirroring the number testing positive. This pattern continued through much of 2023, underscoring the region’s historically high linkage efficiency. However, beginning in 2024 and becoming more pronounced in 2025, the gap between those testing positive and those linked to care begins to widen. While both indicators decline in 2025, the decline in linkage volumes is steeper, resulting in a lower linkage-to-care ratio compared to previous years. This deviation aligns with the period during which the region experienced reduced funding support, particularly support that had historically strengthened case management, follow-up, and patient navigation functions. The weakening of these services likely contributed to reduced follow-up capacity and fewer successful linkages, reflected in the divergence observed in the 2025 data.","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Comparison Between HIV-Positive Cases and Those Linked to Care, Northern Region"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":true,"hideFigureSubCaption":true,"hideFigureFootnote":true,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"3abb711f-f664-4d3a-855a-4e83f1fe86c1","moduleId":"m003","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}}],[{"type":"text","markdown":"The Savanna Region displays a similar overall trajectory but with more pronounced deviations at key points. Through 2020–2022, Savanna mirrors the national pattern relatively well, when positive cases rise, linkage numbers follow closely, indicating functional referral and case management processes. However, the alignment in Savanna is not as tight as what is seen nationally. Where the differences become clearer is in 2023 and especially 2024. Nationally, linkage remains stable even as the number of HIV-positive cases increases, showing that most regions are able to maintain follow-up efficiency during high case volumes. In Savanna, however, the gap widens visibly during this same period: positive cases peak sharply in early and mid-2024, but linkage does not increase at the same rate. This divergence is larger than the gap observed at the national level, pointing to temporary bottlenecks, likely related to capacity constraints, fewer community-based follow-ups, or reduced case tracking efficiency. By 2025, both national and regional trends show a decline in positive cases and linkage volumes. The national ratio remains stable, but in Savanna, the linkage-to-care ratio remains slightly below the earlier years. This indicates that although Savanna partially recovered from the 2024 gap, it did not fully return to the stronger alignment seen between 2020 and 2022.","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Comparison Between HIV-Positive Cases and Those Linked to Care, Savanna Region"}}

By 2023 and 2024, as national patterns fluctuate with noticeable dips in linkage, Upper West maintains relatively steady performance, with linkage volumes tracking closely behind the number of people testing positive. Even where volumes begin to taper slightly in late 2024 and 2025, the proportional relationship between testing and linkage remains stronger than the national pattern, indicating that the region retains a higher level of fidelity in connecting clients to care. Overall, Upper West outperforms the national average by sustaining higher linkage efficiency across the entire review period. The national system shows a stronger ability to convert new diagnoses into care enrollment, while Western North exhibits a consistent, but comparatively lower linkage rate. This gap of roughly 8–10 percentage points highlights the need to strengthen follow-up systems, patient navigation, and facility capacity in Western North as the region continues to experience rising HIV diagnoses.","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Comparison Between HIV-Positive Cases and Those Linked to Care, Western North Region"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":true,"hideFigureSubCaption":true,"hideFigureFootnote":true,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"127a5142-f778-4f85-84e5-1233376e23ed","moduleId":"m003","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}}],[{"type":"text","markdown":"Between January and September 2025, the Western Region maintained a relatively strong proportion of HIV-positive individuals who were successfully linked to care. This stability is particularly notable given the national pattern, where linkage rates slipped slightly in early 2025 before recovering toward mid-year. The Western Region’s ability to retain a high linkage-to-care proportion despite fluctuations in HIV-positive case detection suggests a well-functioning referral and follow-up system. Compared with the national picture, where widening gaps were observed in some quarters, driven largely by regions facing operational or funding pressures, Western Region shows a more resilient service delivery structure. This resilience reflects stronger continuity in care coordination, provider engagement, and patient follow-through.","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Comparison Between HIV-Positive Cases and Those Linked to Care, Western Region"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","markdown":"The pattern of positive HIV cases linked to care shows generally stable performance over time, but with clear pockets of both surpluses and disruptions when actual volumes diverge from expected trends. Early in 2020, the system experienced notable surpluses, with actual linkage volumes exceeding expected levels, possibly reflecting recovery from earlier reporting lags or intensified case-finding efforts. {"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Resilient Service Areas in 2025

Across maternal, newborn and HIV services, the system showed strong continuity within the first three quarters of 2025, with service use staying within expected ranges from January through September. ANC attendance, skilled delivery, postnatal care and live births all followed historical patterns without any significant dips, pointing to stable service availability and consistent care-seeking. Although this stability is encouraging, the underlying quality of care, particularly for maternal and newborn outcomes, will require further assessment to understand whether the maintained volumes reflect true service effectiveness. HIV services also demonstrated clear resilience, with testing, positivity and linkage-to-care trends aligning with long-term expectations across regions. The sector appears to have fully recovered from disruptions observed in earlier years, with HIV service delivery showing dependability and continuity throughout the review period.

# Service Areas Showing Disruption in 2025

Unlike maternal and HIV indicators, malaria treatment services showed a marked disruption beginning in July 2025 and continuing through September. Treatment volumes for both under-five children and individuals aged five and above dropped below expected levels despite stable trends in suspected and tested malaria cases. Because the decline in treatment did not coincide with a reduction in malaria testing or case detection, the pattern suggests a breakdown in the treatment pathway rather than a lower disease burden. The consistency of this dip across regions points toward systemic causes, such as ACT stock challenges, delays in commodity distribution, facility-level workflow bottlenecks or shifts in care-seeking, that require close examination.

# Call to Action

The review highlights a health system that is largely resilient but facing an emerging risk in malaria treatment continuity. An immediate investigation into the causes of the mid-year malaria treatment decline is needed to prevent widening service gaps and potential increases in morbidity. At the same time, the stability in maternal and HIV services offers a strong platform for deeper quality-of-care assessments to ensure that high volumes translate into high-quality outcomes. Strengthening quarterly analytics and rapid review mechanisms will help detect service dips earlier and support timely decision-making across all program areas."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Executive Summary "}}

Between 2020 and 2022, the region exhibited strong linkage outcomes, with the number of clients linked to care closely mirroring the number testing positive. This pattern continued through much of 2023, underscoring the region’s historically high linkage efficiency. However, beginning in 2024 and becoming more pronounced in 2025, the gap between those testing positive and those linked to care begins to widen. While both indicators decline in 2025, the decline in linkage volumes is steeper, resulting in a lower linkage-to-care ratio compared to previous years. This deviation aligns with the period during which the region experienced reduced funding support, particularly support that had historically strengthened case management, follow-up, and patient navigation functions. {"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"Ahafo 🟡: Ahafo demonstrates mostly stable testing across the period but with recurring modest dips. Surpluses appear periodically, especially in 2024–2025, but disruptions during earlier years keep the region in the moderate-performance category. Overall, testing is close to expected but not fully stable.

North East 🔴: North East shows strong seasonal responsiveness but marked volatility. Surpluses during peaks are offset by clear disruptions during low-season troughs. Although the region performs well during high-demand periods, inconsistency across seasons places it in the yellow band.

Northern 🟡: Northern Region handles high testing volumes with relatively strong performance but exhibits multiple disruptions in 2022–2023. Improvements in 2024–2025 are visible, but instability during transition months prevents it from earning a green rating.

Oti 🟢: Oti is one of the most stable performers. Testing aligns closely with expected trends, with mild fluctuations but no extended disruptions. Surpluses in peak seasons further reinforce consistency. Overall, the region demonstrates strong testing reliability.

Savannah 🟢: Savannah shows significant volatility with repeated peaks followed by substantial dips, particularly in 2021–2023. Although improvement is visible in 2024–2025, the region experiences more disruptions than surpluses across the whole period. Service consistency remains a challenge.

Upper East 🔴: Upper East has high testing volumes but experiences prolonged disruptions during multiple years, especially 2022–2023. Although some recovery appears later, persistent dips below expected thresholds and recurrent instability justify a red rating.

Upper West 🔴: Upper West displays recurring disruptions across both peak and low seasons, with several years of performance below expected trends. Although some surpluses occur, they are insufficient to offset sustained volatility. The region requires targeted support.

Western 🟡: Western Region is among the most stable performers, with minimal extreme fluctuations and consistent testing volumes across years. Small deviations occur but do not significantly affect overall performance. Testing closely follows expected seasonal patterns."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Disruptions and surpluses in malaria testing services, Regional Performance"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1.25,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# - Suspected & Tested Malaria Cases

Suspected malaria cases in 2025 closely mirror the historical seasonal pattern observed from 2020–2024. Testing volumes have consistently matched suspected case volumes, demonstrating stable diagnostic capacity nationwide.

No signs of reduction in community health-seeking or facility-level diagnostic performance in 2025. The strong alignment between suspected and tested cases suggests that test-before-treat policy adherence remains intact.

# - Treatment Services (Under 5 Years & 5+ Years)

Both treatment pathways show normal performance up to mid-2025. From July to September 2025, treatment volumes fall below expected levels for both children under five and older age groups. The disruption is visible across multiple months and does not reflect typical seasonal fluctuations. Since suspected and tested cases remained stable, the drop in treatment cannot be explained by changes in disease burden or diagnostic trends.

# - Interpretation of the Treatment Gap

The July - Sept 2025 decline, VERY LIKELY signals a specific disruption in service delivery, not in detection or reporting. Potential drivers that require investigation. The synchronized drop across both age categories strengthens the likelihood of a systemic or supply-chain–related issue rather than a regional or age-specific anomaly."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Malaria – Key Takeaways (Jan 2020 – Sept 2025)"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"North East 🔴— North East experienced a clear and persistent disruption in treatment services during this period. From early 2025 into the middle of the year, actual service use consistently trailed below expected levels, signaling operational or supply challenges that were not quickly resolved. Small surpluses appeared occasionally, but they were short-lived and insufficient to counterbalance the prolonged underperformance. The region’s trajectory indicates a substantial gap in treatment delivery that requires investigation and corrective support.

Northern 🔴— In the Northern Region, treatment services showed a pattern of instability with significant dips beginning in early 2025. Although performance improved marginally mid-year, the region struggled to maintain consistency and often fell below expected values. The lack of sustained recovery suggests ongoing operational strain, possibly linked to the broader challenges previously noted in the region’s health service delivery. Performance during this period skews more toward disruption than stability.

Oti 🟡— Oti demonstrated a mixed performance, with treatment levels alternating between slightly above and slightly below expected trends. While small disruptions were visible in the early months of 2025, they were neither deep nor prolonged. The region stabilised as the year progressed, closing the observation period with treatment volumes that aligned reasonably well with expectations. The overall picture is one of moderate variability but generally acceptable service continuity.


Upper East 🟡— Upper East exhibited a sustained decline in treatment service delivery, beginning in late 2024 and continuing throughout the first half of 2025. The region consistently failed to meet expected treatment levels, with no notable periods of recovery. This prolonged underperformance points to structural or supply-side challenges that may be affecting access or utilization. The magnitude and duration of the disruption make Upper East one of the weakest performers during this period.

Upper West 🔴— Upper West showed moderate performance challenges across late 2024 into mid-2025. Although there were some periods of stability and isolated surpluses, treatment volumes dipped repeatedly below expected trends, particularly during the early part of 2025. The disruptions were not as prolonged as those seen in Upper East or North East, but the pattern indicates systemic strain rather than isolated fluctuations. Recovery toward Q3 2025 was partial and inconsistent.


Western North 🟡— Western North encountered persistent challenges in meeting expected treatment volumes across the entire period. From late 2024 well into 2025, service levels remained below expected trends with only isolated and short-lived surpluses. The absence of a sustained rebound points to chronic disruptions in treatment delivery, potentially linked to supply, staffing, or service access issues. The region’s performance reflects one of the most consistently disrupted patterns nationally."}]]}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":true,"hideFigureSubCaption":true,"hideFigureFootnote":true,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"37b275b6-952c-4cdf-95aa-dbf886f01bf2","moduleId":"m001","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}},{"type":"text","span":5,"markdown":"The completeness of reported HIV data from 2020–2025 is generally low across all indicators and regions, with every value falling below the 80% threshold. Reporting on babies receiving ARV prophylaxis is especially weak, with most regions submitting fewer than one-third of expected reports, and Upper East showing the lowest completeness at only 15.8%.  Similarly, linkage into HIV care, HIV testing among the population, and HIV testing coverage show substantial gaps, with completeness typically ranging between 30–60%. Even regions that tend to perform better on other health indicators, such as Greater Accra, Volta, and Bono, still fall short of acceptable completeness standards in this dataset. The consistently low levels across all regions and indicators suggest major challenges in routine reporting systems for malaria-related HIV indicators, including delayed submissions, non-reporting facilities, or systemic issues with DHIMS2 data entry workflows. \nThis level of incompleteness limits the reliability of the dataset for monitoring performance, identifying service gaps, or guiding interventions, and highlights the need for targeted data-quality strengthening efforts at facility and district levels.","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}]],"useFooter":false,"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Completeness of Reported HIV Data, 2020 - 2025"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":true,"hideFigureSubCaption":true,"hideFigureFootnote":true,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"eec4952d-afc6-42c5-a544-52ec7935b428","moduleId":"m002","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}},{"type":"text","span":5,"markdown":"The completeness adjustment reveals substantial upward corrections across all HIV service indicators, indicating that a considerable portion of service volumes was initially under-reported. The largest proportional increases are seen in babies on ARV prophylaxis, where nearly all regions recorded extreme adjustments, ranging from about 85% in Bono to over 400% in the Upper East, suggesting major data gaps in routine reporting for PMTCT services. Similarly, linkage into HIV care shows large upward shifts in several regions, especially the North East, Oti, and Savannah, where adjustments exceed 100%, pointing to widespread under-capture of entry-point data. For population-level HIV testing and positivity, the adjustments are more moderate but still significant: many regions required increases of 20–60%, with Upper East, Upper West, and Savannah surpassing 100% for HIV-positive cases. These patterns imply that both testing and treatment cascades were substantially underestimated in the raw data. Even the indicator with the smallest corrections, Number Tested for HIV (Population), still shows increases of 15–80% across most regions. While the volume of positive tests remains steady and even trends slightly upward, linkage does not rise at the same pace, resulting in a larger shortfall than what was observed in earlier years. This shift suggests that the system’s ability to transition newly diagnosed individuals into treatment has weakened in 2025, despite stable testing performance. {"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"Ahafo 🟡 — In Ahafo, HIV testing volumes had been below expected levels for a long stretch from 2021 through mid-2024, but the trend turns positive from late 2024. By mid-2025, the region shows a clear surplus, suggesting strengthened outreach, improved test kit availability, or renewed case-finding momentum.

North East 🟡 — North East remains a low-volume region, and the testing pattern from late 2024 through 2025 is relatively stable with modest fluctuations. While there is no severe disruption, the region does not show strong surpluses either. Testing remains broadly aligned with expected levels, though the small rise in early 2025 suggests some service strengthening.

Northern 🔴— Northern Region shows a slight downturn in testing activity toward late 2024, dipping below expected levels, but this short disruption is followed by a stabilization in early 2025. By mid-year, testing performance returns closer to expected levels, though the region does not achieve strong surpluses. Performance is moderate and somewhat fragile.

Oti 🟡 — Oti continues its characteristic oscillations, with several small dips below expected values from late 2024 into 2025. Though the declines are not sharp, they represent persistent instability rather than a clear improvement. Testing levels do recover intermittently, but performance remains inconsistent.

Savannah 🟢 — Savannah’s HIV testing pattern between late 2024 and mid-2025 is relatively flat. Testing stays close to expected levels, neither dropping significantly nor showing strong recovery. The region demonstrates stable but unremarkable performance without clear surpluses.

Upper East 🟢 — Upper East experiences noticeable disruptions beginning in late 2024 and continuing into 2025, with prolonged periods where testing lags below expected levels. This underperformance is more pronounced than in most other regions, suggesting supply-chain issues, workforce gaps, or disruptions in routine testing points. Recovery is limited by Sept 2025.

Upper West 🟢 — Upper West shows a period of weaker performance in late 2024 where testing dips below expected levels. Early 2025 remains subdued, but a slight recovery appears mid-year. However, the rebound is small, and overall performance remains modest and slightly below expectations.

Western 🟢 — Western Region maintains generally stable testing through late 2024 with brief dips. In early–mid 2025, testing levels improve and begin to approach expected volumes again. While there is still volatility, the overall direction trends toward recovery, indicating steadying service delivery.

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"- Overall HIV service trends remain broadly stable across testing, positivity, and linkage to care, with quarterly patterns in 2024–2025 following historical expectations.  Testing volumes have recovered from the disruptions seen in 2022–2023, and positivity levels remain consistent with long-term patterns.


- Short, temporary disruptions continue to appear in specific parts of the cascade, especially during mid-2022 to early-2023. These include dips in testing, positivity, and linkage-to-care volumes falling below expected levels for a few months at a time. While not prolonged, they signal operational fluctuations at facility level.

- Data completeness for HIV indicators remains critically low across all regions, with most indicators falling far below the 80% completeness benchmark. Reporting gaps, especially for ARV prophylaxis for newborns, population HIV testing, and linkage to care, significantly limit the reliability of the dataset and complicate interpretation of performance trends.

- Regional performance shows improvement in some areas but still exhibits substantial variability. Regions such as Greater Accra, Bono, Western, and Volta perform relatively better in testing and linkage trends but still show major data completeness gaps. Regions like Upper East, Oti, and Northern consistently under-report key HIV indicators.

- Despite data limitations, linkage-to-care volumes demonstrate resilience and generally align with expected patterns through 2024 and 2025, indicating stable continuity-of-care efforts at national level.

- Overall interpretation should be made cautiously due to persistent reporting weaknesses, which likely obscure true service coverage, mask facility-level disruptions, and highlight the need for systematic strengthening of DHIMS2 reporting workflows."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"HIV Service Delivery: Key Takeaways"}}

Ashanti is the only region with a double-digit rise in PNC1 coverage (10.7%), indicating considerable improvement once completeness is accounted for. Conversely, regions such as Ahafo, Bono, Oti, Volta, Western, and Bono East show small adjustments (mostly 1–4%), reflecting relatively stable routine reporting systems with fewer missing records. Overall, the adjustments reveal that while data completeness affects all regions to some extent, the magnitude of the impact is highly variable, with northern and savannah-belt regions generally showing larger corrections than coastal and forest zones.","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Percentage Change in RMNCAH-N Service Volume due to completeness Adjustment"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1.25,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"Across the continuum of maternal health services, the disruption analysis shows a generally resilient system with short-lived fluctuations rather than sustained declines. ANC, which is more sensitive to repeated service contact, also demonstrated strong stability with only small patches of surplus or minor disruptions; this suggests that once women entered ANC, many were able to continue their follow-up visits despite external pressures. Delivery services followed a similar pattern, seasonal fluctuations persisted, but the service never sustained deep or prolonged disruptions. Instead, occasional surpluses in late 2024 and 2025 indicate improved access or reporting completeness during those months. PNC services mirrored the delivery pattern, with stable utilisation and only a modest surplus emerging in 2025, again hinting at improved postpartum follow-up or data completeness. \n\nLive births continued to show the strongest consistency of all indicators, with utilisation patterns tracking very closely to expected levels and only a moderate surplus emerging in early–mid 2025. Taken together, these trends highlight that core maternal health service use, including ANC initiation, ANC completion, delivery care, and postnatal follow-up, has remained largely robust across the five-year period, with no evidence of systemic declines in the number of women accessing these services. The stability and improvements observed in 2025 across multiple indicators suggest strengthened service continuity, greater community confidence in care, and improved reporting completeness. However, while routine service-use data indicate resilience on the demand side, they cannot reveal potential deterioration in service quality, particularly in the context of recent funding constraints that may affect commodities, staffing, and facility readiness. For this reason, follow-up investigation through the health-facility survey is essential to determine whether the quality of maternal care has been impacted despite stable utilisation. "}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Key Highlights - RMNCAH-N Service Delivery"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"Ahafo 🟡: In Ahafo, malaria treatment volumes for older children and adults remained largely aligned with expected trends throughout late 2024, with only minor fluctuations. As the system moved into 2025, actual service use stayed close to seasonal expectations, showing neither pronounced surpluses nor significant disruptions. Overall, the region maintained a steady pattern of delivery, suggesting stable facility operations and consistent treatment-seeking behaviour.

North East 🔴: North East displayed relatively stable performance in late 2024, with actual service volumes tracking expected levels despite minor dips. In early to mid-2025, however, a small downturn emerged, marked by short-lived disruptions that brought actual volumes slightly below expected trends. While these deviations were not severe, they signal emerging pressures on service continuity that may warrant closer monitoring.

Northern 🔴: The Northern Region showed a generally predictable seasonal pattern through late 2024, but early 2025 brought a measurable decline in treatment volumes relative to expected levels. Although not extreme, this downward shift persisted across several months, indicating a meaningful disruption. Given Northern’s large population and persistent service demand, this trend suggests potential challenges such as stockouts, reduced facility attendance, or operational delays.

Oti 🟡: Oti maintained stable malaria treatment volumes through the end of 2024, aligning well with expected patterns. Entering 2025, the region experienced intermittent but noticeable dips, particularly around mid-year, where disruptions lowered service use below expected levels. The pattern suggests emerging inconsistencies in service access or supply availability that were not present earlier in the period.

Savannah 🔴: Savannah exhibited moderate surpluses in late 2024 before transitioning into a more erratic pattern in 2025. Although treatment volumes remained generally close to seasonal expectations, small disruptions appeared mid-2025, indicating possible interruptions in service delivery. Despite these declines, Savannah’s performance remained comparatively more stable than several neighbouring regions.

Upper East 🟡: Upper East showed relatively weak performance from late 2024 into 2025, with multiple months of actual service use falling below expected levels. The region experienced recurring disruptions across the first half of 2025, representing one of the clearest signs of sustained underperformance. This persistent gap highlights a potential lapse in supply chains, reporting reliability, or care-seeking patterns requiring follow-up.

Upper West 🔴: Upper West ended 2024 with service use closely following expected levels; however, early 2025 saw disruptions that kept actual volumes consistently below trend. Some recovery appeared mid-2025, but the rebound did not fully restore performance to expected levels. The repeated shortfalls suggest structural or logistical constraints affecting treatment continuity during this period.



"}]]}}
